name: Unofficial Well-Architected MAS MindForge Lens
description: A custom AWS Well-Architected Lens that maps the MAS Project MindForge AI Risk Management and Governance Framework (January 2026) into the AWS Well-Architected Tool. This lens helps Financial Services Institutions assess their AI governance posture across four domains: AI Oversight, AI Risk Management, AI Lifecycle Management, and Enablers. It is unofficial and community-maintained; always refer to the official MAS handbook for authoritative guidance.

questions.title: How do you define the scope and application of your AI governance framework?
questions.description: Define what constitutes AI within your institution. Establish clear boundaries, proportionality, and materiality thresholds. Ensure the scope covers internal, vendor-developed, and third-party AI systems. The handbook recommends three general considerations when defining AI: nature of the AI system, role of human involvement, and materiality of the use case.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Scope and Application guidance

choices.title: AI is formally defined; scope covers all systems with proportionality; materiality thresholds are documented and applied

improvementPlan.displayText: Periodically review the AI definition and scope. Update materiality thresholds as AI capabilities evolve. Ensure new AI use cases are automatically assessed against scope criteria.

choices.title: AI is partially defined or scope covers only some AI systems; materiality thresholds are informal or inconsistent

improvementPlan.displayText: Formalise the AI definition using the three general considerations (nature of system, human involvement, materiality). Expand scope to cover vendor and third-party AI. Document materiality thresholds.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Start by defining what constitutes AI in your institution. Use the MAS MindForge handbook's three general considerations. Inventory known AI systems and apply a basic materiality assessment.

questions.title: How do you define and assign responsibilities for AI oversight?
questions.description: Leverage existing enterprise governance functions rather than creating new standalone AI governance structures. Ensure the Board, Senior Management, and operational governance have clear AI responsibilities. Establish operating effectiveness measures and horizon-scanning for emerging AI risks.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - AI Oversight responsibilities guidance

choices.title: Three lines of defence model applied to AI with clear Board, Senior Management, and operational roles; operating effectiveness is measured

improvementPlan.displayText: Conduct periodic reviews of AI governance operating effectiveness. Update horizon-scanning processes to capture emerging AI risks (e.g. Agentic AI).

choices.title: Some AI oversight roles defined but gaps in Board/Senior Management accountability, operating effectiveness, or horizon-scanning

improvementPlan.displayText: Formalise AI responsibilities across the three lines of defence. Ensure Board and Senior Management have explicit AI risk accountability. Implement operating effectiveness measures.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Map AI oversight responsibilities to existing governance structures. Start with a RACI matrix for Board, Senior Management, and operational teams. Define minimum operating effectiveness measures.

questions.title: How do you ensure effective AI-related policies, procedures, and standards?
questions.description: Develop AI governance documents that define AI within your institution, establish AI identification processes, classify AI use cases by risk materiality, and set periodic review cadences. The handbook emphasises that the AI definition is a foundational governance document that must be reviewed and updated.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - AI Policies guidance

choices.title: AI policies formally define AI, include identification/classification processes, classify by risk materiality, and are reviewed periodically

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance policies with emerging AI type definitions (e.g. Agentic AI). Automate identification and classification workflows. Link policy reviews to regulatory updates.

choices.title: Some AI policies exist but lack formal AI definition, identification process, or risk materiality classification

improvementPlan.displayText: Develop a formal AI definition. Establish an AI identification process and classify use cases by risk materiality. Set a periodic review schedule for all AI governance documents.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Draft an initial AI policy that defines AI in your institution and establishes an identification process. Use the MAS MindForge handbook's guidance on AI definition and classification.

questions.title: How do you enhance organisation-level AI risk management?
questions.description: Integrate AI risk into your enterprise risk management framework. Define AI risk taxonomies, risk appetite, and tolerances. Establish Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) and incident tracking mechanisms specific to AI.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Organisation-Level AI Risk Management guidance

choices.title: AI risk is fully integrated into ERM with defined taxonomies, appetite, tolerances, KRIs, and incident tracking

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance AI risk reporting with predictive KRIs. Conduct stress-testing for AI-specific scenarios. Expand incident tracking to include near-misses and model failures.

choices.title: AI risk is partially integrated into ERM but lacks taxonomies, KRIs, or formal incident tracking

improvementPlan.displayText: Define AI-specific risk taxonomies and appetite thresholds. Establish KRIs for AI systems. Implement formal AI incident tracking and reporting.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Map AI risks to your existing enterprise risk taxonomy. Define initial AI risk appetite and establish a basic KRI dashboard. Start tracking AI incidents.

questions.title: How do you uplift practices for managing third-party AI risks?
questions.description: Implement pre- and post-procurement mitigations for third-party AI. Conduct due diligence, establish contractual safeguards, and use AI Cards for vendor disclosure. Monitor third-party AI throughout the engagement lifecycle.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Third-Party AI Risk guidance

choices.title: Third-party AI risk framework covers pre/post procurement, due diligence, contractual safeguards, AI Cards, and ongoing monitoring

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance third-party monitoring with automated compliance checks. Expand AI Card requirements to all critical vendors. Conduct periodic third-party AI risk audits.

choices.title: Some third-party AI controls exist but lack systematic pre/post procurement framework, AI Cards, or ongoing monitoring

improvementPlan.displayText: Build a systematic third-party AI risk framework covering pre-procurement due diligence and post-procurement monitoring. Introduce AI Cards for vendor disclosure. Strengthen contractual safeguards.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Inventory all third-party AI services. Conduct initial due diligence and establish minimum contractual safeguards. Start requiring basic AI disclosure from vendors.

questions.title: How do you enhance use case-level AI risk management?
questions.description: Conduct inherent risk materiality assessments for each AI use case. Apply risk tiering (e.g. low, medium, high, critical). Map controls from the MindForge Library of Guardrails. Assess residual risk after controls are applied.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Use Case-Level AI Risk Management guidance

choices.title: All AI use cases have inherent risk materiality assessments, risk tiering, mapped controls from the Library of Guardrails, and documented residual risk

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance use case assessments with scenario analysis and stress testing. Integrate with enterprise risk reporting. Automate control gap identification.

choices.title: Some use cases have risk assessments but lack formal risk tiering, Library of Guardrails mapping, or residual risk analysis

improvementPlan.displayText: Extend inherent risk materiality assessments to all AI use cases. Apply formal risk tiering. Map controls from the MindForge Library of Guardrails. Document residual risk.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Start with the highest-materiality use cases. Conduct inherent risk materiality assessments using the MindForge AI Risk Taxonomy. Use the AI Card template to structure assessments.

questions.title: How do you ensure AI inventory capabilities?
questions.description: Maintain an AI inventory with core attributes covering system identification, ownership, risk materiality, and dependencies. Assign clear roles: Use Case Owner, Inventory Owner, and AI-specific reviewer. Keep the inventory up to date through tooling and periodic reconciliation.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - AI Inventory guidance

choices.title: AI inventory has core attributes, clear roles (Use Case Owner, Inventory Owner, reviewer), is kept up to date, and is integrated with existing asset management

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance inventory with lineage tracking and automated change detection. Integrate with model registries and CI/CD pipelines. Implement automated discovery for shadow AI.

choices.title: Partial AI inventory exists but lacks core attributes, formal roles, regular updates, or integration with asset management

improvementPlan.displayText: Define core attributes for the AI inventory. Assign formal roles (Use Case Owner, Inventory Owner, reviewer). Establish quarterly reconciliation. Integrate with existing asset management.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Create a baseline AI inventory starting with known high-risk systems. Define core attributes and assign ownership. Establish a quarterly update cadence.

questions.title: How do you manage use case context and design?
questions.description: Assign a Use Case Owner for every AI system. Conduct a preliminary inherent risk materiality assessment before design. Establish go/no-go checkpoints. Design human oversight mechanisms: Human in the Loop (HITL), Human over the Loop (HOTL), or Human out of the Loop (HOOL).

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Use Case Context and Design guidance

choices.title: Every AI use case has a Use Case Owner, preliminary inherent risk assessment, go/no-go checkpoints, and designed human oversight (HITL/HOTL/HOOL)

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance design processes with bias analysis and fairness metrics. Integrate ethical review into go/no-go checkpoints. Document human oversight rationale.

choices.title: Some use cases have owners and risk assessments but lack go/no-go checkpoints or formal human oversight design

improvementPlan.displayText: Mandate Use Case Owners for all AI systems. Implement preliminary inherent risk assessments. Establish go/no-go checkpoints. Design human oversight (HITL/HOTL/HOOL) for each use case.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Start by assigning Use Case Owners to known AI systems. Conduct preliminary inherent risk assessments. Establish basic go/no-go criteria. Document intended human oversight approach.

questions.title: How do you manage data acquisition and processing for AI?
questions.description: Assess data fitness (quality, representativeness, balance, and ethical alignment). Address third-party data compliance (IP, licensing, usage rights). Document metadata and lineage. Implement bias mitigation in training and test data. Consider synthetic data where appropriate.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Data Acquisition and Processing guidance

choices.title: Data fitness is assessed for all AI datasets; metadata/lineage is documented; bias mitigation is implemented; third-party data compliance is verified

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance data governance with automated fitness checks and real-time bias detection. Expand provenance to third-party and synthetic data. Implement continuous data quality monitoring.

choices.title: Some data controls exist but gaps in fitness assessment, metadata/lineage, bias mitigation, or third-party data compliance

improvementPlan.displayText: Implement formal data fitness assessments for all AI datasets. Document metadata and lineage. Establish bias mitigation procedures. Verify third-party data compliance (IP, licensing).

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Start with data inventory and classification for AI systems. Implement basic data quality checks. Document metadata for high-risk datasets. Establish data ownership.

questions.title: How do you manage onboarding, build, and review of AI systems?
questions.description: Establish processes for selecting pre-trained models, fine-tuning, model validation, and testing. Implement AI-specific disclosure and user transparency requirements. Apply tiered review approaches based on risk materiality.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Onboarding, Build, and Review guidance

choices.title: Structured build process covers pre-trained model selection, fine-tuning, validation, testing, AI-specific disclosure, and tiered review

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance build process with automated testing and continuous validation. Integrate model cards and disclosure documentation. Implement principle-based review frameworks.

choices.title: Some build controls exist but lack structured model selection, disclosure requirements, or tiered review

improvementPlan.displayText: Formalise the AI build process with pre-trained model selection criteria, fine-tuning standards, and validation protocols. Implement AI-specific disclosure requirements. Establish tiered review based on risk materiality.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Define minimum build standards for AI. Implement model validation and basic testing before deployment. Start with disclosure requirements for customer-facing AI.

questions.title: How do you manage AI system deployment?
questions.description: Establish deployment decision points, approval workflows, and production readiness criteria. Implement staged deployment (e.g. shadow, pilot, limited, full). Ensure rollback capabilities are tested and documented.

choices.title: Deployment pipeline has decision points, approval workflows, production readiness checks, staged deployment, and tested rollback

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance deployment with canary releases and automated rollback triggers. Integrate deployment checks into CI/CD. Add post-deployment validation gates.

choices.title: Some deployment controls exist but lack formal decision points, staged deployment, or tested rollback

improvementPlan.displayText: Build a structured deployment pipeline for AI with formal decision points and approval workflows. Implement staged deployment (shadow, pilot, limited, full). Test and document rollback procedures.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Define minimum deployment controls for AI. Establish a staging environment and basic readiness checklist. Document rollback procedures.

questions.title: How do you manage AI system usage, monitoring, and change?
questions.description: Implement post-deployment monitoring including performance, drift, fairness, and security. Establish human oversight over-the-loop for high-risk decisions. Implement output validation, feedback loops, incident management, and formal change management. Plan for model retirement.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Usage, Monitoring, and Change Management guidance

choices.title: Continuous monitoring covers performance, drift, fairness, and security; human oversight over-the-loop is implemented; change management and incident response are formalised

improvementPlan.displayText: Enhance monitoring with automated alerting and model retraining triggers. Integrate change management with model registry. Implement formal model retirement and decommissioning procedures.

choices.title: Some monitoring exists but lacks drift detection, formal human oversight over-the-loop, structured incident response, or change management

improvementPlan.displayText: Implement continuous monitoring for all production AI systems including drift and fairness metrics. Establish human oversight over-the-loop for high-risk decisions. Formalise incident response and change management.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Start with basic performance monitoring for production AI. Define change management process for model updates. Establish incident response procedures for AI failures.

questions.title: How do you enable AI governance with skills, knowledge, and culture?
questions.description: Build AI literacy and risk awareness through tiered training (e.g. Board/Senior Management, practitioners, all staff). Develop skills for key roles. Foster a culture of responsible AI use. Implement approved usage policies for AI tools.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - Skills, Knowledge, and Culture guidance

choices.title: Tiered AI literacy program covers Board, practitioners, and all staff; key roles have defined skills; approved usage policies are in place

improvementPlan.displayText: Expand training to emerging AI topics (e.g. Agentic AI). Measure and report on culture and awareness metrics. Update approved usage policies as AI tools evolve.

choices.title: Some AI training exists but is not tiered, lacks coverage for key roles, or approved usage policies are missing

improvementPlan.displayText: Develop tiered AI literacy curricula for Board, practitioners, and all staff. Define skills requirements for key roles. Implement approved usage policies for AI tools.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Start with awareness training for leadership and AI practitioners. Develop an approved usage policy for commonly used AI tools. Build towards role-based curricula.

questions.title: How do you manage AI infrastructure?
questions.description: Ensure infrastructure supports AI workloads securely and scalably. Address compute considerations for traditional AI, Generative AI, and Agentic AI. Implement security controls and access management. Consider environmental impact and sustainability.

helpfulResource.displayText: MAS Project MindForge - AI Infrastructure guidance

choices.title: AI infrastructure covers traditional, Gen AI, and Agentic AI workloads with security controls, scalability, access management, and environmental considerations

improvementPlan.displayText: Optimise infrastructure costs with auto-scaling and efficient compute allocation. Enhance security with zero-trust networking. Measure and report environmental impact.

choices.title: Some infrastructure controls exist but gaps in Gen AI/Agentic AI support, security, access management, or environmental considerations

improvementPlan.displayText: Assess infrastructure requirements for Gen AI and Agentic AI workloads. Implement security controls and access management. Establish environmental impact monitoring.

choices.title: None of these

improvementPlan.displayText: Assess current AI infrastructure against security and scalability requirements. Establish baseline controls for AI compute and data storage. Implement access management.
